10.1016/j.jmp.2018.10.004
Likert scales or more generally rating scales are one of the most used tools of data collection in psychology. Every now and then an occasionally heated debate arises on the scale type of these data. Just as an example of this kind of argument we refer to  Carifio and Perla (2007, 2008) ,  Jamieson (2004) , and  Norman (2010) . In the present paper we do not intend to add another polemical paper to the already existing ones but rather to investigate the structure of the set of all possible rating scales. Surprisingly, a fairly interesting mathematical object emerges from this investigation.

Occasionally, in the literature the distinction is made between a Likert item and a Likert scale, where the scale consists of a number of carefully selected items. In cases this distinction is neglected leading to a lot of confusion. To steer clear of this confusion we emphasize that in the present paper a rating scale is a well defined object, namely an element of  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                      , a set which will be defined in Section  2 .

The issue of scale type cannot be resolved without taking into account the empirical system from which the rating scale data are obtained. Thus, the paper  Jamieson (2004)  with its numerous rules and guide lines cannot capture the gist of a measurement theoretic tack on the problem.

In the 1980ies the focus of scale type discussions shifted from the Stevens concept of  admissible transformations  to the approach via  automorphisms  championed by Narens. One of the problems with admissible transformations is a rigorous definition. Automorphisms are isomorphic self mappings of a given structure. In this context the structure under scrutiny is the relational system describing the objects or persons under investigation. Two properties of the structures came to the fore: degree of uniqueness and of homogeneity. This approach culminated in the seminal Alper/Narens theorem. See  Alper (1985, 1987)  and  Narens (1981, 1985) . The development is comprehensively expounded in  Luce, Krantz, Suppes, and Tversky (1990, Chap. 20) . For a generalization of the Alper/Narens theorem see  Ovchinnikov (2001)  and  Suck (2000) .

This theorem has a simple interpretation: It is impossible to define a scale type which is more restrictive than ordinal scales and less restrictive than interval scales. For rating scales this seems bad news. Often we have the feeling that data obtained from rating scales contain more information than merely an ordering of the rated items; but arguments to regard them as interval scaled are rather weak. They seem to lie ‘somehow in between’.

In the present paper we will suggest a way around this dilemma. In the sequel, we take a long way employing algebra, order theory, and topology to come up with a kind of back door which avoids the normative power of the Alper/Narens theorem. Our very last theorem ( Theorem 21 ) and the subsequent interpretation opens up a new perspective on this problem.

In the following sections we will describe the mathematical properties of these sets from an algebraic (Section  2 ), order theoretic (Section  3 ), and topological (Section  4 ) point of view. These aspects are closely interrelated. In Section  5  we describe the interconnections in more detail. The question of scale type will be taken up in Section  6 . The paper ends with a short discussion including some open problems. Here we will briefly take up the distinction between items and scales.

                         a 
                         =

                            4 
                            )

                         R 
                      .

A structure satisfying  (a)  is a  semigroup . Our semigroup has neutral element  
                         ω 
                      . Such a structure is sometimes called a monoid. Our monoid is commutative, furthermore it satisfies idempotence, i.e., condition  (d) .

The proofs of  (c) and (d)  are omitted because they are almost obvious.  □

Theorem 2  imposes an algebraic structure on the (equivalence classes of the) rating scales. A semigroup appears to be not much of a structure; however, it will be useful when we regard  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       from the viewpoint of order theory. This is the topic of the next section.

Finally, we add another property of our semigroup which is not shared by semigroups in general.

≤ 
                             is a partial order on  
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∼ 
                                  
                               
                            . Reflexivity and antisymmetry of  
                               ≤ 
                             are obvious. For transitivity let

                               α 
                               ≤ 
                               γ 
                            .

To show that this partial order is in fact a lattice we have to demonstrate that the operations given in  (5) and (6)  have the properties of supremum and infimum. Let  
                               γ 
                               ≥ 
                               α 
                               , 
                               β

                                  (

                            . Thus  
                               α

                             is the supremum of  
                               α

                               β

For the infimum we first observe that  
                               α 
                               ∧ 
                               β 
                             is well defined. Note the fact that the number of components of  
                               b

Theorem 4  provides an order structure for the (equivalence classes of the) rating scales. Surprisingly, we have much more structure than a mere partially ordered set. If need be all the beautiful results on distributive lattices are at our disposal. The next theorem points out another property concerning the interplay of semigroup addition and the lattice operations.

Theorem 6  will be utilized in Section  4.2 ; there we will exploit these properties to define a metric.

We mention in passing that the subsemigroups defined at the end of Section  2  give rise to sublattices. Possibly, the sublattice  
                         
                            
                               E 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       is enough to deal with rating scales in practice.

In this section we will endow  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       with a topological structure. We shall discuss two approaches; the first is combinatorial in nature in so far as we count the number of components which belong to only one rating scale when comparing two; the second can take on values in a continuum; the actual values of the components are utilized in this approach. There is a third approach which we will shortly present in the  Appendix . It proves to be a failed attempt; but the idea behind it seemed obvious and apparently it bears some potential which might be useful in other contexts.

For the sake of completeness we give the definition of a metric.

These conditions imply

                         0 
                      . The third condition is generally known as the triangle inequality.

A metric space is a topological space. Thus, questions of continuity can be asked and answered in it. Topological spaces seem to be too general for our purposes in this paper because the notion of ‘closeness’ has no meaning in a topology.

We will show that we can define a natural and useful metric by counting components. The idea is to regard equivalence classes of rating scales as close to each other if there are representatives which share many or almost all components. On the other hand those  
                            α 
                            , 
                            β 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                          should be regarded far separated if no representatives can be found which have many components in common. More precisely, we define

                                        , i.e. they are non negative and satisfy symmetry and the triangle inequality.

Proof 
                            First we note that the definition of  
                                  d 
                                depends on  
                                  α 
                                and  
                                  β 
                               , not on  
                                  a 
                                and  
                                  b 
                               , because given the constraints on  
                                  a 
                                and  
                                  b 
                               , they are unique for  
                                  α 
                                and  
                                  β 
                               , respectively. 
                            Clearly,  
                                  d 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     α 
                                     , 
                                     α 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  0 
                               , and if  
                                  d 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     α 
                                     , 
                                     β 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  0 
                                then there is some  
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  α 
                                  ∧ 
                                  β 
                               , hence  
                                  α 
                                  = 
                                  β 
                               . Symmetry in  
                                  α 
                                and  
                                  β 
                                in the definition guarantees  
                                  d 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     α 
                                     , 
                                     β 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  d 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     β 
                                     , 
                                     α 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . To verify the triangle inequality we pick  
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  α 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  β 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  γ 
                                such that the first and the last component of all three are the same, i.e.,  
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                     
                                        k 
                                     
                                  
                               . For arbitrary finite sets  
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  Z 
                                we have  
                                  
                                     (8) 
                                     
                                        
                                           | 
                                           X 
                                           ▵ 
                                           Y 
                                           |

                                           | 
                                           X 
                                           ▵ 
                                           Z 
                                           |

                                           | 
                                           Z

                                           | 
                                        
                                        .

                                  C

                                     a

                                  C

                                     b

                                  C

                                              b

                                  d

                                  d

                                  d

For a better understanding of the metric we describe an open ball of radius  
                            r 
                          in  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                         . Let  
                            α 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                         . Then the set of rating scale equivalence classes which has a distance from  
                            α 
                          of less than  
                            r 
                         
 consists of all those classes  
                            β 
                          which have a representative  
                            b 
                            ∈ 
                            β 
                          such that they differ in less than  
                            r 
                          components from some representative from  
                            α 
                          with matching endpoints.

The topology on  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                          generated by this metric is however not very interesting from the viewpoint of a topologist. Since  
                            d 
                          is integer-valued any one-element subset of  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                          is open, hence every subset being the union of its one-element subsets is open.

The clue to a suitable definition of a metric which takes into account the numerical values of the differences between the components of two rating scales comes from Section  3 , in particular  Theorem 6 . In short, we endow the covering graph of

                                     ∼

                               ≤ 
                               )

The following theorem is well known in graph theory. Since  
                            G 
                          is infinite and weighted we do not have a proper reference; therefore its proof is included here.

Theorem 13 
                            
                               
                                  D 
                                
                               is a metric on  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               .

D 
                                
                               is a metric on  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               .

Proof 
                            The first two conditions of  Definition 7  are obvious. For the triangle inequality we pick  
                                  ε 
                                  > 
                                  0 
                               , and paths  
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     α 
                                     , 
                                     γ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     γ 
                                     , 
                                     β 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                such that  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           L 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    p 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    1 
                                                 
                                              
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ≤ 
                                           D 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              α 
                                              , 
                                              γ 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           + 
                                           ε 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           L 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    p 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    2 
                                                 
                                              
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ≤ 
                                           D 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              γ 
                                              , 
                                              β 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           + 
                                           ε 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               Let  
                                  p 
                                denote the path in  
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     α 
                                     , 
                                     γ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                identical with  
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                until it reaches  
                                  γ 
                               , then identical with  
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               . Clearly, we have  
                                  L 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     p 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  L 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  + 
                                  L 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               , hence  
                                  
                                     
                                        D 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           α 
                                           , 
                                           β 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ≤ 
                                        L 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           p 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ≤ 
                                        D 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           α 
                                           , 
                                           γ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        D 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           γ 
                                           , 
                                           β 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        2 
                                        ε 
                                        . 
                                     
                                  
                               Since  
                                  ε 
                                is arbitrary the triangle inequality follows.  □

The next example shows that even when the vectors representing  
                            α

                            β

                            D 
                          are different:

In this section we combine the structures derived on the set of rating scales in the preceding three sections.

The partial order  
                         ≤ 
                       defined in  (3)  is closely connected to the semigroup operation; it is defined drawing on the + operation between rating scales. Thus, the properties derived in  Theorem 4  are consequences of this kind of addition.

For the interrelation of the metrics with the algebraic structures we have to answer the question: Is  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       a topological semigroup, i.e., given the metric the question of continuity of the operation + on  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       is to be answered.

This question is easily dealt with in the case of the metric  
                         d 
                      . The product topology on  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                         × 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       is as simple as the topology on  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       arising from the metric. A singular point in  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                         × 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                      , say  
                         
                            ( 
                            ξ 
                            , 
                            η 
                            ) 
                         
                       forms an open set because it is the Cartesian product of the two circles with radius  
                         
                            
                               1 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       around  
                         ξ 
                       and  
                         η 
                      , respectively. Thus, this set is open, hence all subsets are open. For a function to be continuous the preimage of an open set in  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       has to be open in the product topology on  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                         × 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                      . Since every set is open in  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                         × 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                      , so is the preimage of +.

We summarize this observation as:  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            d 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a topological commutative monoid. Admittedly, this is not very telling, not more at least as saying the non negative integers are a commutative topological monoid.

The metric space  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            D 
                            ) 
                         
                       requires a little more. However, the result is the same. We formulate it in a separate theorem.

Theorem 19 
                         
                            The metric  
                            
                               D 
                             
                            gives rise to a topology on  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  + 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            such that + is a continuous operation on  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∼ 
                                  
                               
                            
                            .

The metric  
                            
                               D 
                             
                            gives rise to a topology on  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  + 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            such that + is a continuous operation on  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∼ 
                                  
                               
                            
                            .

                                     α 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     β 
                                  
                                  
                                     1

                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     β 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            , respectively, such that for  
                               α 
                               , 
                               β 
                             in the respective neighborhoods  
                               α 
                               + 
                               β 
                             is in the neighborhood of  
                               
                                  
                                     α 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     β 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            . It suffices to restrict this argument to balls, i.e., for each  
                               r 
                               > 
                               0 
                             there are  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               > 
                               0 
                             such that for all  
                               α 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           r 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             and  
                               β 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           r 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        β 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             the sum  
                               α 
                               + 
                               β 
                             is in  
                               
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  + 
                                  
                                     
                                        β 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            . We defer the choice of  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             to the end. 
                         Thus,  
                               α 
                             and  
                               β

Concluding this section on the interrelation of the algebraic and topological properties of  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       we remark that a topology based on the partial order  
                         ≤ 
                      , the so called interval topology is very different from the topology derived from the metric  
                         D 
                      . To see this notice that each element of an interval has elements in each of its neighborhoods which do not belong to the interval. Thus, no interval is open in the topology derived from  
                         D 
                      .

The question of scale type was one of the incentives to undertake the present investigation. The constructions of the preceding sections seem to point to sort of a reconciliation of the dilemma outlined in the introduction. Loosely speaking, we try to circumvent the limitations the Alper/Narens theorem imposes by including the measurement instrument, i.e., the rating scale into the measurement structure. This way measurement becomes an interaction between measured objects, persons, and properties which make up the relational structure.

Keeping this aspect in mind we propose the following reasoning: A transformation of rating scale data is admissible if it is strictly increasing and transforms a rating scale  
                         α 
                       into a scale  
                         
                            
                               α 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                       which is close in the sense of the metric  
                         D 
                      , i.e.,  
                         D 
                         
                            ( 
                            α 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  α 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                         < 
                         r 
                       for some  
                         r 
                         > 
                         0 
                      . Clearly, for closeness in this sense we can utilize different graduations, i.e., different values of  
                         r 
                      . We will explain this argument in the sequel and underpin it with a few mathematical results.

We start by defining a mapping from  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       into  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       which is generated from an increasing transformation of the data.

We mention in passing that Lipschitz continuity implies continuity. More results of how properties of  
                         f 
                       transfer to properties of  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       can possibly be derived.

The idea of the interpretation of  Theorem 21  in the context of admissible transformation is the following: Transformations of rating scale data are admissible if applied to the rating scale the ensuing rating scale is close to the original scale. Thus,  Theorem 21  (c) restricts the set of admissible transformations. However, (b) is also a restriction on admissible transformations because continuity of  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       implies a restriction on  
                         f 
                       since not all increasing functions are continuous.

Theorem 21  (c) can be utilized to classify admissible transformations according to the Lipschitz-constant  
                         K 
                      . The smaller  
                         K 
                       the more restrictive is the condition. More precisely:

Let  
                         Lip 
                         
                            ( 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       be the set of monotonic functions from  
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ] 
                         
                       onto  
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ] 
                         
                       satisfying Lipschitz continuity with the constant  
                         K 
                      . Then  
                         
                            
                               Lip 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               Lip 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                               
                                for  
                               
                               
                               K 
                               ≤ 
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      Thus we have a hierarchy from interval scales to ordinal scales by means of the classes  
                         Lip 
                         
                            ( 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Other hierarchies and refinements can be found and defined with the help of similar results if it is possible to transfer these properties to properties of  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                      .  Theorem 21  may be just the beginning of a fruitful development.

These properties can facilitate the choice between rating scales.

Finally, a few remarks on further aspects of the preceding development should be made.

Rating scales in practice . We are fully aware of the fact that an applied scientist who uses rating scales as a means of gathering data is confronted with questions which we did not address in this paper. Should the marks consist of numbers or words or anchors? How many marks? Sometimes it is stated that the wording of the marks has an influence on the scale type of the results. Perhaps such a statement can be examined using some of our concepts. But we did not investigate this question. In the early days of scaling theory techniques were discussed which should make an estimation of the difference between marks of a rating scale possible (law of categorical judgment cf.  Torgerson (1958) ). This was done in extension of Thurstonean scaling. In the context of random utility theory these techniques were investigated under an axiomatic viewpoint, cf.  Suck (1997) . Recently, a series of papers has been published which investigate axiomatic properties the linguistic marks of a rating scale should have to allow for an evaluation of the differences between them. This approach is similar to Torgerson’s law of categorical judgment; however the employed technique is more in line with measurement theory, cf.  García Lapresta, González del Pozo, and Pérez-Román (2018) ,  García Lapresta and Pérez-Román (2015) ,  García Lapresta and Pérez-Román (2018)  and the literature cited in these papers.

Occasionally, it might be useful to regard the rating 0 as invariant; for example in rating scales such as  
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            2 
                            , 
                            − 
                            1 
                            , 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      . In such cases, it might be more sensible to introduce the concept of  ratio-equivalent  rating scales. We could define  
                         a 
                         
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         b 
                       if and only if  
                         ∃ 
                         r 
                         ∈ 
                         R

                         0 
                       such that  
                         r 
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               b 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         . 
                       The respective set of equivalence classes,  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ∼ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                       contains a one-element sequence as neutral element (this element should be  
                         ≠ 
                         0 
                      ). Results similar to the ones presented here can be derived for  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ∼ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      . The necessary changes are quite obvious in all cases.

Distinction between items and scales.  In the introduction we briefly mentioned the distinction between Likert items and Likert scales. The present paper did not contribute to this discussion. However, in this section a few remarks seem appropriate. Construction of scales is a time honored method in the development psychological tests. Often rating scales (in the sense of our development here) are employed for the items. The techniques which lead to psychometric scales where several items are carefully selected to form in combination a scale seem to have the power to form something new according to  Carifio and Perla (2007) . They use analogies from chemistry (atoms versus molecules) and even from nuclear physics (quarks) to describe the properties scales have. How these properties emerge is rather mysterious. If such a process is really present its underlying dynamics should be the object of investigation and not just an unfounded claim. In such an investigation the development presented here can be useful.

However, scale construction in the psychometric sense is prone to dangerous pitfalls. To give an example, assume a perfect Guttman scale, i.e.,  
                         n 
                       items which can be answered or solved by two possibilities, 1 and 0, meaning correctly solved or failed, or agreed or disagreed. In the context of the present paper we say that the items are measured by the rating scale  
                         ω 
                      . The Guttman scale is perfect if the items can be ordered such that a person solving an item can also solve all items preceding this item in this order. Assuming some innocuous regularity conditions the correlation matrix consists of the entries  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           i 
                                           ⋅ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              n 
                                              + 
                                              1 
                                              − 
                                              j 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                           ⋅ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              n 
                                              + 
                                              1 
                                              − 
                                              i 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      It was observed by  Zwick (1987)  that the eigenvalues of this matrix  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ≥ 
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                         ≥ 
                         …

                       are  
                         
                            (14)

                                        i

                                        1 
                                        )

She does not give a proof and it seems it is still an open problem;  Davis-Stober, Doignon, and Suck (2015)  determined the eigenvalues and the eigenvectors of the corresponding covariance matrix. However, although unsolved, numerical evidence can easily be provided; it shows  (14)  true for all  
                         n 
                       in reach of your computer. Depending on the criterion you refer to the usual psychometric scale construction techniques suggest up to  
                         
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       factors, implying the same number of scales can be extracted. For example, you can extract up to 7 scales from 50 items. All these factors are of course nuisance factors — you started with a perfect Guttman scale, so there is only one scale.

In a quite different context  Schweitzer and Troche (2018)  also found and discussed factors which are hard to explain with regard to content.

Distinction between the rationals and the reals.  In our definitions in Section  2  we made no distinction between  
                         Q 
                       or  
                         R 
                       as ground set for rating scales. In the following sections this was unimportant. All the results hold for both cases. However, when it comes to questions of topological connectedness or completeness this distinction is crucial. Thus, when these properties are investigated the definition of  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       should be explicit with respect to this ground set.

Open problems.  In the  Appendix  we formulate a third approach to a distance between rating scales. One can speculate whether this approach can be extended to yield a useful metric. The basic idea seemed natural but the obstacles proved high. That does not exclude that this idea might lead to another useful metric.

It is possible, even probable that results similar to  Theorem 21  can be derived. They can lead to refinements of the  
                         Lip 
                         
                            ( 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       classification.

A problem which kind of suggests itself is whether  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       is continuous for continuous  
                         f 
                      . In other words, is  Theorem 21  (a) invertible?

The order  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       was shown to be a distributive lattice. Which other properties does it have? We want to mention a peculiarity which is encountered in many cases where lattice theory is applied: One of the two operations  
                         ∨ 
                       and  
                         ∧ 
                       appears natural (in our case  
                         ∧ 
                      ) while the other is a bit involved. We mention two examples: The face lattice of a polytope has a natural infimum (intersection of two faces) while the supremum is not so straightforward. For knowledge spaces the supremum is natural (union of two states) while the infimum is more complicated.

How complicated is the calculation of  
                         D 
                         
                            ( 
                            α 
                            , 
                            β 
                            ) 
                         
                       for large

                         D 
                         
                            ( 
                            α

                            β

On the practical side it seems an interesting question whether the structure of our  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               ∼ 
                            
                         
                       can be brought to bear on the questions of scaling linguistic rating scales in the sense of  García Lapresta, González del Pozo et al. (2018) .

In this appendix we present a failed attempt to define a metric. This attempt tries to exploit the obvious idea to draw on the vector space metric in  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                      . Clearly, two elements  
                         α 
                         , 
                         β 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            
                               R

                               R 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       for some  
                         n 
                      ; in general, this  
                         n 
                       is different for  
                         α 
                       and  
                         β 
                      , but the smaller one can be regarded as a subspace of the larger. So, why not utilize the well-known metric of the Euclidean vector space  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                      ? This appendix gives an answer.

The metric of Section  4.1  does not give rise to an interesting topology. Moreover, it does not discriminate between rating scales for which the components are different although close to each other. For example for

We prefer to take both, calculate the Euclidean distance between the longer and both the elongated vectors and take the geometric mean as their distance. More precisely we introduce the following

Although this approach seems to have failed, there is at least one way to proceed from here. One can define a topology on
